heading: Brag Sheet
content:
# Brag Sheet

> Add to this immediately when something good happens. Don't rely on memory.
> Format: date, what happened, context, any numbers or names worth noting.
> This feeds your resume bullets, performance reviews, and LinkedIn posts.

---

heading: How to use this
content:
## How to use this

1. **Add wins as they happen** â€” a sentence or two is enough
2. **Review quarterly** â€” pull the best entries into your resume or LinkedIn
3. **Use it in performance reviews** â€” your manager will thank you
4. **Mine it before interviews** â€” it's your story bank

---

heading: Ongoing
content:
## Ongoing

**Started 2025 â€” Co-founded Ri-Tech, a technology company delivering client projects across Ghana**
Co-founded Ri-Tech with a partner, serving as Co-Founder & CTO. The company has delivered 12+ projects across web development, mobile apps, networking, and branding for real clients including KGL Group, Allied Ghana, Bonvas Tours, and St. Joseph's Catholic Hospital. Built and maintain the company's own portfolio site using Next.js and Sanity CMS. The team has grown to 5 members covering development, networking, design, and PR.

**Key outcome:** 12+ live client projects Â· 5-person team Â· multiple industries served Â· 100% client satisfaction rate

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Represented the team Â· Went above and beyond_

---

heading: Dec 2025â€“Present
content:
### Dec 2025â€“Present

**Dec 2025â€“Present â€” Built a working prototype of a smart laundry pickup/dropoff kiosk (hardware + software)**
Designed and built a full IoT laundry kiosk system â€” like an Amazon locker, but for laundry â€” from scratch with a co-founder who provided resources and hardware. Solely responsible for all technical development across four layers: embedded hardware communication, Android kiosk app, backend API, and admin dashboard.

**Hardware:** Integrated an AL1645 TCP/IP locker controller board to control a 16-locker cabinet (6 small, 6 medium, 4 large). Initially attempted RS485 serial communication â€” spent significant time troubleshooting direct serial and USB-to-RS485 adapters to establish communication with the Android device. After a board failure during testing, switched to a TCP/IP-based controller which resolved the communication issues entirely. Identified and worked around a mechanical constraint: the locker cabinet (modified school lockers) uses flexible sheet metal that requires the unit to be fixed in place for doors to latch reliably.

**End-to-end flow:** Customer walks up â†’ selects drop-off â†’ enters mobile number â†’ verifies OTP â†’ selects service and locker size â†’ locker opens automatically â†’ customer places items â†’ system detects door closure and creates an order (status: CREATED). Staff collects items via admin panel, processes the order, and creates an invoice sent to the customer via SMS with a Paystack payment link. When ready, customer receives an SMS with a unique pickup code. If unpaid at pickup, the kiosk displays a QR code to complete payment before the locker opens.

**Stack:** Flutter (Android kiosk app) Â· NestJS (backend API) Â· Next.js (admin dashboard) Â· Paystack Â· SMS notifications Â· AL1645 TCP/IP locker controller  
**Status:** Working prototype built and tested with real users indoors. Next step: rewire and deploy publicly at a university campus.

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Solved a hard problem Â· Learned something significant Â· Went above and beyond_

---

heading: March
content:
### March

**Mar 2026 â€” Resolved non-interactive CI/CD sudo permissions blocking production deployments**
While migrating a client's Laravel laundry POS from cPanel to a self-managed VPS, set up a GitLab CI/CD pipeline with separate staging and production environments. Hit a hard blocker: the deployment user needed sudo access to restart Supervisor (used for Laravel job queues), but the CI pipeline is non-interactive and couldn't accept a password prompt. Spent 1â€“2 days debugging and learning, ultimately configuring targeted sudoers permissions for the deploy user to resolve it without compromising server security. Strengthened hands-on knowledge of Linux server administration, Nginx, Supervisor, and GitLab CI/CD.

_Categories: Solved a hard problem Â· Learned something significant_

---

heading: January
content:
### January

**Jan 2026 â€” Delivered corporate website for Allied Ghana (Allied Oil) after nearly a year of client iterations**
Built the full corporate website for Allied Ghana (alliedghana.com), a Ghanaian fuel and lubricants company operating since 1998. Received a Figma design and implemented everything from frontend to Sanity CMS â€” including product/services pages, a station locator, fuel pricing display, the Allied+ loyalty card section, and partnership content (Afton Chemical, Castrol, Toyota, Mobil). Project came as a direct referral from the KGL Group client via Paper Merchant. Navigated a long client feedback cycle with frequent design changes across nearly a year, staying professional and responsive throughout until final delivery.

**Stack:** Next.js, Sanity CMS, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS  
**Key outcome:** Live corporate site Â· referral client Â· delivered despite ~1 year of design change cycles

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Solved a hard problem Â· Got recognised (referral)_

---

**Jan 2026 â€” Built and currently maintaining a multi-branch laundry POS system for a live client**
Independently designed and developed a full-featured point-of-sale system for a laundry business, currently in active testing with a real client. Features include order management (full CRUD), customer management, multi-branch support, analytics, Paystack payment integration, expense tracking, internal ledger, and order dispatching to track warehouse workflow. Implemented a multi-channel notification system (email, SMS, WhatsApp) where the channel is selected based on each customer's preference. Orders trigger automatic notifications with a payment link. Supports partial payments. Planning to evolve into a full SaaS product.

**Stack:** Laravel, React (Vite), Inertia.js, Paystack API  
**Key outcome:** Live with a paying client Â· multi-branch Â· partial payments Â· 3-channel notifications

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Solved a hard problem Â· Learned something significant_

---

heading: August
content:
### August

**Aug 2025 â€” Built and delivered a full-stack e-commerce storefront for a live Ghanaian kitchenware brand**
Designed and developed Kitchen Comfort (kitchen-comfort.com), a production e-commerce platform for a kitchenware client in Ghana. Features 62 products across 8 categories, product variants, sale pricing, stock management, a full admin dashboard for managing products and orders, Paystack payment integration, Cloudinary for image storage, and Zoho Mail for transactional email. Handed over to client after completion. The store is live and has since processed 45 orders, with 23 delivered.

**Stack:** Next.js, Prisma ORM, PostgreSQL (Neon), Paystack, Cloudinary, Zoho Mail  
**Live site:** kitchen-comfort.com  
**Key outcome:** 62 products Â· 45 orders processed Â· 23 delivered Â· fully live in production

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Hit a number Â· Went above and beyond_

---

heading: July
content:
### July

**Jul 2025 â€” Launched a live permit management platform now serving 1,687 students and processing 165,100 GHS**
Designed and developed a full-stack permit management system for my university's Student Representative Council (SRC) as my final year project. MVP launched July 2025 and has been continuously updated and maintained since. Handles role-based authentication, permit issuance, payment collection, and entry verification at exam halls. Navigated a real-world payment integration challenge: Paystack required formal business documents the SRC didn't have â€” after back-and-forth negotiations, secured starter access, hit their 30,000 GHS lifetime limit in the first semester alone, then migrated to ExpressPay (which required an official school authorisation letter to unlock API access). The system is live, has processed 165,100 GHS across two semesters, and is used by 1,687 registered students. School administration endorsed the project. Will be handed over to the incoming SRC administration upon graduation.

**Stack:** Next.js, Prisma ORM, MySQL, ExpressPay API  
**Key outcome:** 165,100 GHS processed Â· 1,687 registered users Â· live in production Â· school endorsement

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Hit a number Â· Got recognised Â· Solved a hard problem Â· Went above and beyond_

---

heading: Early 2025
content:
### Early 2025

**Early 2025 â€” Built corporate website for KGL Group, one of Ghana's top-ranked ICT conglomerates**
Delivered the full corporate website for KGL Group (kglgroup.com.gh), a major Ghanaian conglomerate with six subsidiaries spanning Fintech, Logistics, Agric, Property Development, and Commerce â€” ranked first in Ghana's ICT sector at the Ghana Club 100 Awards. Received a Figma design from Paper Merchant (a brand solutions agency) and built everything from scratch: frontend, multi-page navigation (subsidiaries, careers, press releases, gallery, sponsorships), and Sanity CMS for content management. Completed in approximately 2 months. The quality of the work led directly to a second referral project â€” Allied Ghana.

**Stack:** Next.js, Sanity CMS, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS  
**Key outcome:** Live enterprise site Â· completed in ~2 months Â· earned direct referral to second client

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Got recognised (referral) Â· Hit a number_

---

heading: Juneâ€“August
content:
### Juneâ€“August

**Junâ€“Aug 2024 â€” Internship at CSIR: built a real-time noise monitoring dashboard with map clustering**
Completed a 3-month internship at the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), a national government research institution. Was tasked with building the frontend for a noise monitoring system â€” a network of IoT devices deployed across the city that measure decibel levels in real time. Built a live dashboard in Next.js that consumed data via MQTT, displayed device locations on an interactive map, showed live dB readings per device, and implemented map clustering to handle future scale. Supervisor (who built the backend) specifically praised the MQTT integration and clustering implementation. This project was also the introduction to Next.js, which became the primary framework used across all subsequent projects.

**Stack:** Next.js, MQTT, map/clustering library  
**Key outcome:** Supervisor praise for MQTT handling and map clustering Â· introduced to Next.js Â· real government research project

_Categories: Shipped something Â· Learned something significant Â· Got recognised_

---

heading: May 2024â€“May 2025
content:
### May 2024â€“May 2025

**May 2024â€“May 2025 â€” Completed ALX Software Engineering Programme (Cohort 17)**
Completed the 12-month ALX Software Engineering programme, one of Africa's most rigorous software engineering courses. Built projects including a full Airbnb clone and a bytecode interpreter for the Monty scripting language written in C â€” demonstrating low-level programming fundamentals beyond typical web development. Graduated with a certificate in May 2025, running the programme concurrently with client work and the permit system build.

**Key outcome:** Certificate earned Â· low-level C programming Â· 12-month structured engineering curriculum

_Categories: Learned something significant Â· Shipped something_

---

<!-- Keep adding entries above in reverse chronological order -->

---

heading: Categories to track
content:
## Categories to track

<!-- Not every win fits neatly â€” use these as prompts when you're not sure what to add -->

- **Shipped something** â€” feature, project, product, campaign
- **Solved a hard problem** â€” technical, organisational, creative
- **Helped someone** â€” mentored, unblocked, supported a colleague
- **Got recognised** â€” praise from a manager, client, stakeholder, or peer
- **Learned something significant** â€” skill, tool, domain knowledge
- **Represented the team** â€” presented, spoke, wrote publicly
- **Improved a process** â€” made something faster, cheaper, more reliable
- **Hit a number** â€” metric, target, milestone
- **Got a testimonial or strong feedback** â€” add to `testimonials.md` too
- **Went above and beyond** â€” did something outside your job description

heading: Bio
content:
# Bio

> Keep 3 versions: a one-liner, a short paragraph, and a full version.
> Copy-paste whichever fits the context.

---

heading: One-liner
content:
## One-liner

<!-- ~15 words. For Twitter bios, email signatures, quick intros. -->

Daud Rahim â€” full-stack developer building products and client software across Ghana.

---

heading: Short bio
content:
## Short bio

<!-- 3â€“5 sentences. For portfolio sites, LinkedIn summary, conference speaker bios. -->

Daud Rahim is a full-stack developer based in Ghana. He builds web applications, SaaS products, and IoT systems for enterprise clients, startups, and institutions â€” from corporate sites for conglomerates like KGL Group to a live permit management platform serving 1,687 university students. Working entirely through referrals, he has delivered 12+ projects across web development, mobile, networking, and branding. He's comfortable across the full stack and increasingly interested in the intersection of software and hardware.

---

heading: Full bio
content: ## Full bio

heading: Background
content:
### Background

Daud started his software journey through the ALX Software Engineering Programme (Cohort 17) â€” one of Africa's most rigorous engineering curricula â€” completing it over 12 months while simultaneously building client products and delivering real production projects. His foundation spans low-level C programming through to modern web frameworks, giving him an unusually broad technical range for a developer at his stage.

heading: What I do
content:
### What I do

Daud builds full-stack web applications, SaaS products, and IoT systems â€” primarily for clients in Ghana, with growing interest in remote and international work. His clients range from large enterprises (KGL Group, Allied Ghana) to civic institutions (university SRC) to early-stage startups. He handles everything from requirements and architecture through to deployment and ongoing maintenance, often as the sole technical person on a project.

heading: How I work
content:
### How I work

Daud's default mode is end-to-end ownership â€” he takes a brief, figures out the architecture, builds it, ships it, and keeps it running. He's comfortable navigating ambiguous requirements, difficult clients, and production problems. Years of delivering projects solo have sharpened his ability to scope work, manage client relationships, and coordinate with designers, networking engineers, and other collaborators.

heading: Recent focus
content:
### Recent focus

Over the last year, Daud has been expanding beyond pure web development â€” building a full IoT laundry kiosk system (hardware integration, Android app, backend API, admin dashboard) and going deeper into DevOps, Linux server administration, and CI/CD pipelines. He's also actively building a laundry POS system he plans to evolve into a SaaS product.

heading: Beyond work
content:
### Beyond work

Daud is interested in how technology can solve practical, everyday problems in the Ghanaian and broader African context â€” not just for large organisations, but for small businesses and institutions that rarely get purpose-built software.

---

heading: Notes on framing

## Notes on framing

- **For a startup:** Emphasise speed, full-stack ownership, shipping fast with small teams, and the SaaS/product angle
- **For an enterprise / agency:** Emphasise client work history (KGL Group, Allied Ghana), CMS expertise, reliability, and long project delivery
- **For a remote tech company:** Emphasise Next.js depth, TypeScript, CI/CD, and the breadth of production projects
- **For IoT / hardware roles:** Emphasise the kiosk build â€” 4-layer architecture, hardware debugging, Flutter + NestJS + TCP/IP integration

heading: Freelance Full-Stack Developer
content:
## Freelance Full-Stack Developer

**Type:** Freelance  
**Period:** 2024 â€“ Present  
**Location:** Ghana (remote-capable)

heading: What the role was
content:
### What the role was

Independent freelance development work across web applications, e-commerce, IoT systems, and institutional platforms. All clients have come through referrals â€” no cold outreach or platforms. Projects span corporate websites, SaaS products, e-commerce, and institutional software. Collaborate with a small network of designers, networking engineers, and a PR person to deliver full-service projects where needed.

heading: What I actually did day-to-day
content:
### What I actually did day-to-day

- Took briefs directly from clients, scoped projects, quoted, and delivered end-to-end
- Architected and built all products â€” from requirements through to deployment and ongoing maintenance
- Managed all client communication â€” feedback cycles, revisions, delivery timelines
- Made all stack and infrastructure decisions independently
- Handled production deployments, VPS setup, CI/CD pipelines, and post-launch support
- Navigated real-world constraints: shifting requirements, payment provider restrictions, tight budgets, hardware failures

heading: What I built or delivered
content:
### What I built or delivered

- **KGL Group website** â€” corporate site for one of Ghana's top-ranked ICT conglomerates (6 subsidiaries, Sanity CMS); completed in ~2 months; led directly to a referral for Allied Ghana
- **Allied Ghana website** â€” corporate site for a Ghanaian fuel and lubricants company (alliedghana.com); referral via Paper Merchant; delivered after ~1 year of client-driven design iterations
- **Kitchen Comfort e-commerce** â€” full production storefront (kitchen-comfort.com); 62 products across 8 categories, Paystack, Cloudinary, full admin dashboard; 45 orders processed, 23 delivered
- **Laundry POS system** â€” multi-branch point-of-sale for a live client; order management, customer management, analytics, Paystack, expense tracking, internal ledger, partial payments, 3-channel notifications (email, SMS, WhatsApp); planned evolution into SaaS
- **Smart laundry kiosk (IoT)** â€” full 4-layer system: AL1645 TCP/IP locker controller, Flutter Android kiosk app, NestJS backend, Next.js admin dashboard; working prototype tested with real users; next step is public campus deployment
- **SRC Permit Management System** â€” full-stack platform for Knutsford University's Student Representative Council; 1,687 registered users, 165,100 GHS processed across two semesters; school administration endorsed; migrated payment provider mid-project from Paystack to ExpressPay after hitting volume limits

content:

heading: Project: SRC Permit Management System
content:
# Project: SRC Permit Management System

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Live â€” actively maintained |
| **Period** | July 2025 â€“ Present |
| **Role** | Solo developer â€” designed, built, deployed, and maintaining |
| **Type** | Solo |
| **Client / Organisation** | Student Representative Council (SRC), Knutsford University |
| **Industry** | Education / Civic |
| **Confidential?** | No â€” school administration endorsed; screenshots available |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

Knutsford University's SRC had no system for managing exam hall entry permits. The process was manual, error-prone, and created bottlenecks at exam hall entry points. Students had no self-service way to obtain permits or pay fees, and the SRC had no visibility into who had paid or been issued a permit. This was also my final year project.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Designed the full system architecture from scratch â€” data model, payment flow, role structure
- Built the complete full-stack application solo: frontend, backend, database, payment integration
- Negotiated directly with Paystack to obtain starter API access for an informal student body that lacked formal business registration documents
- Managed the migration from Paystack to ExpressPay mid-production after hitting Paystack's 30,000 GHS lifetime limit in the first semester â€” required obtaining an official school authorisation letter to unlock ExpressPay API access
- Launched the MVP in July 2025 and have continuously updated and maintained it across two semesters
- Coordinated handover planning with the incoming SRC administration

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Next.js (frontend + server-side rendering)
- Prisma ORM
- MySQL
- ExpressPay API (migrated from Paystack)

**Approach / methodology:**

Built as a full-stack Next.js application with role-based authentication covering three user types: students, SRC staff, and exam hall verifiers. Students register, pay, and receive permits through a self-service flow. Exam hall staff verify entry using the system at the door. The SRC admin panel gives full visibility into registrations, payments, and permit status.

Payment integration required unusual legwork â€” negotiated Paystack starter access for an unregistered student body, hit their GHS lifetime cap mid-semester, then navigated ExpressPay's onboarding which required a formal letter from the university. Both migrations were done without taking the system offline.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Registered users | 1,687 | Across two semesters |
| Total payments processed | 165,100 GHS | Two semesters combined |
| Paystack limit hit | 30,000 GHS | First semester alone â€” triggered migration |
| Uptime | Continuous | No major downtime across two semesters |

**In plain language:**
The system replaced a fully manual process with a self-service platform used by nearly 1,700 students. It processed over 165,000 GHS in payments across two semesters and earned a formal endorsement from the school administration. It will be handed over to the incoming SRC on graduation.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

- The SRC had no formal business registration â€” getting any payment provider to work required negotiation and creative problem-solving
- Hitting the Paystack lifetime cap mid-semester meant migrating payment providers while the system was live with active users
- Navigating two different payment provider onboarding processes under time pressure
- Maintaining the system across two semesters as a student while completing coursework

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

Getting the payment infrastructure to work at all. Most developers would have hit the "no business registration" wall and stopped. Instead I negotiated access, used it fully, migrated cleanly to a second provider, and kept the system running throughout. That's not just a technical win â€” it's a problem-solving and persistence win.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

I'd design the payment provider abstraction layer properly from the start, so swapping providers wouldn't require touching as much code. A clean payment interface would have made the Paystack â†’ ExpressPay migration much faster.

---

heading: Artifacts & evidence
content:
## Artifacts & evidence

| Type | Description | Link / Location | Public? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Screenshots | UI screenshots available | See `artifacts/` folder | Yes |
| Live system | Active during exam periods | â€” | Restricted to students |

---

heading: How to pitch this project
content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a full-stack Next.js + MySQL permit management system with role-based auth, self-service student flows, and real-time entry verification. Navigated a live payment provider migration from Paystack to ExpressPay mid-production â€” triggered by hitting volume limits â€” without downtime. Maintained across two semesters as a solo developer.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
Replaced a manual exam entry process with a self-service platform used by 1,687 students, processing over 165,000 GHS in fees. Delivered as a final year project and formally endorsed by the school administration.

heading: One-line version

heading: Project: Smart Laundry Pickup/Dropoff Kiosk
content:
# Project: Smart Laundry Pickup/Dropoff Kiosk

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Working prototype â€” awaiting public deployment |
| **Period** | December 2025 â€“ Present |
| **Role** | Sole technical developer â€” all four layers |
| **Type** | Team (2 people: me + co-founder who provided resources/hardware) |
| **Client / Organisation** | Internal / startup project |
| **Industry** | IoT / Laundry / Consumer tech |
| **Confidential?** | Partially â€” concept is public, business details are not |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

Laundry businesses have no convenient, self-service way for customers to drop off and pick up items outside of staffed hours. The goal was to build a smart kiosk â€” similar to an Amazon locker â€” that lets customers drop off laundry, pay remotely, and collect items using a unique code, all without staff involvement at the point of handoff.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Designed the full 4-layer system architecture: hardware control â†’ Android kiosk app â†’ backend API â†’ admin dashboard
- Integrated the AL1645 TCP/IP locker controller board to control a 16-locker cabinet (6 small, 6 medium, 4 large)
- Built the Android kiosk app in Flutter â€” locked-down, public-facing, runs the full customer drop-off and pickup flow
- Built the NestJS backend API handling order creation, status tracking, OTP verification, and payment
- Built the Next.js admin dashboard for staff to manage orders, process laundry, create invoices, and trigger locker opens
- Wired up SMS notifications and a Paystack payment link flow triggered at invoice creation
- Debugged and resolved hardware communication issues â€” initially attempted RS485 serial, switched to TCP/IP after identifying the board mismatch
- Identified and documented a mechanical constraint: the modified school locker cabinet requires fixed mounting for doors to latch reliably

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Flutter (Android kiosk app â€” locked-down public mode)
- NestJS (backend API)
- Next.js (admin dashboard)
- AL1645 TCP/IP locker controller board
- Paystack (payment links)
- SMS notifications (OTP + pickup codes)

**Approach / methodology:**

The system works across four distinct layers that all have to work together reliably. The customer flow runs entirely on the kiosk: select drop-off â†’ enter mobile number â†’ verify OTP â†’ choose service and locker size â†’ locker opens â†’ customer places items â†’ door closure is detected â†’ order created (status: CREATED).

Staff manage the rest through the admin panel: collect items, process laundry, create an invoice sent to the customer by SMS with a Paystack payment link. When ready, the customer gets an SMS with a unique pickup code. If payment is incomplete at pickup, the kiosk displays a QR code to complete payment before the locker opens.

Hardware integration required significant debugging. Initially attempted RS485 serial communication via USB-to-RS485 adapter â€” spent substantial time troubleshooting before the controller board failed during testing. Switched to the AL1645 TCP/IP-based controller which resolved the communication issues entirely.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Lockers controlled | 16 | 6 small, 6 medium, 4 large |
| Layers built | 4 | Hardware, Android app, API, dashboard |
| Real user testing | Yes | Tested indoors with real users |
| Status | Prototype complete | Rewiring and campus deployment next |

**In plain language:**
A fully working smart laundry kiosk â€” the only thing standing between prototype and public deployment is rewiring and a fixed installation point. The entire system was built solo from hardware integration up to the admin dashboard.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

- Hardware debugging with no prior locker controller experience â€” had to learn the RS485 protocol, troubleshoot adapters, deal with a board failure, and switch approaches entirely
- Coordinating four independent technical layers that all need to work in sync under real-world conditions
- A mechanical constraint (flexible sheet metal cabinet) that required identifying and documenting a physical installation requirement before public deployment
- Building a Flutter kiosk app in locked-down Android mode for untrusted public use

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

The end-to-end system working as a coherent product â€” not just individual parts. The moment a customer walks up, drops off their laundry, pays by SMS link, and collects using a code with no staff involved is genuinely novel for this context. Getting the hardware and software to talk reliably, especially after the RS485 dead end, was a real technical win.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

Start with TCP/IP hardware from the beginning â€” the RS485 attempt cost significant time. Also, I'd design the cabinet with fixed mounting as a hard requirement from day one rather than discovering it during testing.

---

heading: Artifacts & evidence
content:
## Artifacts & evidence

| Type | Description | Link / Location | Public? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Video recording | Prototype demo recording | See `artifacts/` folder | Partially |
| Live link | Admin dashboard | â€” | No |

---

heading: How to pitch this project
content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a full IoT laundry kiosk system across four layers: AL1645 TCP/IP locker controller integration, Flutter Android kiosk app (locked-down public mode), NestJS backend API, and Next.js admin dashboard. Handled hardware communication debugging (RS485 â†’ TCP/IP pivot), OTP flows, Paystack payment links, and SMS notifications end-to-end. Working prototype tested with real users.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
Built a smart self-service laundry dropoff and pickup kiosk â€” like an Amazon locker, but for laundry. Customers drop off items, receive a payment link by SMS, and collect using a unique code. No staff needed at the point of handoff. Working prototype ready for deployment.

heading: One-line version
content:
### One-line version
> Designed and built a full IoT laundry kiosk system across hardware, Android app, backend API, and admin dashboard â€” working prototype tested with real users.

---

heading: Tags
content:
## Tags

`iot` `hardware` `flutter` `android` `nestjs` `next.js` `tcp-ip` `locker-controller` `paystack` `sms` `kiosk` `solo` `prototype` `ghana`

heading: Project: Laundry POS System
content:
# Project: Laundry POS System

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Live â€” active testing / soft launch with paying client |
| **Period** | January 2026 â€“ Present |
| **Role** | Solo developer â€” designed, built, deploying |
| **Type** | Solo |
| **Client / Organisation** | Confidential â€” laundry business in Ghana |
| **Industry** | Retail / Laundry / SaaS (planned) |
| **Confidential?** | Yes â€” client identity confidential |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

A laundry business with multiple branches had no centralised system to manage orders, customers, staff, payments, and finances. Operations were tracked manually, making it impossible to get visibility across branches or enforce consistent workflows. No existing off-the-shelf POS product fit the specific workflow of a laundry business.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Designed the full data model and system architecture for a multi-branch POS
- Built the complete application solo: frontend, backend, database, integrations
- Implemented multi-branch support â€” each branch operates independently but reports to a central admin view
- Built a full order dispatching system to track laundry through warehouse workflow stages
- Implemented a multi-channel notification system where the channel (email, SMS, or WhatsApp) is selected per customer based on their preference
- Integrated Paystack with support for partial payments
- Built an internal ledger and expense tracking module
- Deployed to a live client currently in active testing

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Laravel (backend)
- React with Vite (frontend)
- Inertia.js (full-stack bridge)
- Paystack API
- Email, SMS, WhatsApp notifications

**Approach / methodology:**

Built as a Laravel + React monolith using Inertia.js, which allows React components to be served server-side without building a separate API. This kept the codebase lean and deployable without complex infrastructure. Multi-branch support is baked into the data model from the start â€” every order, customer, and transaction is scoped to a branch, with a super-admin view across all branches.

The notification system checks each customer's preferred channel before sending â€” so a customer who prefers WhatsApp gets WhatsApp, not SMS. Orders trigger automatic notifications with a payment link on creation. Partial payments are tracked against the order total with a running balance.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Branches supported | Multi | Architecture supports unlimited branches |
| Notification channels | 3 | Email, SMS, WhatsApp â€” per customer preference |
| Payment support | Partial payments | Running balance tracked per order |
| Client status | Live / soft launch | Active testing with paying client |

**In plain language:**
A fully custom POS system built specifically for laundry businesses, live with a real paying client. Handles everything from order intake to payment to warehouse dispatch across multiple branches. Planning to evolve into a SaaS product.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

- No off-the-shelf reference for how a laundry POS should work â€” had to design the domain model from scratch
- Multi-channel notifications required careful per-customer preference logic and multiple API integrations
- Partial payment tracking adds complexity to the order lifecycle â€” a single order can have multiple payment events
- Designing for multi-branch from day one while keeping the codebase maintainable as a solo developer

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

The notification system â€” automatically routing to the right channel per customer with no manual intervention is a genuinely thoughtful UX detail. Most small business software sends everything by one channel. Getting WhatsApp, SMS, and email working with per-customer preference routing was more complex than it sounds.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

I'd invest more time upfront defining the warehouse workflow stages with the client before building â€” the dispatch/tracking module required the most iteration because the workflow wasn't fully clear at the start.

---

heading: Artifacts & evidence
content:
## Artifacts & evidence

| Type | Description | Link / Location | Public? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live system | Client deployment | â€” | No â€” confidential |

---

heading: How to pitch this project
content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a Laravel + React (Inertia.js) multi-branch laundry POS with Paystack partial payment support, a per-customer 3-channel notification system (email, SMS, WhatsApp), order dispatching for warehouse workflow tracking, and an internal ledger. Live with a paying client. Designed from the start as a foundation for a future SaaS product.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
A custom point-of-sale system built for a laundry business with multiple branches. Handles orders, customers, payments, warehouse dispatch, and finances in one place. Live with a real client, with plans to turn it into a product other laundry businesses can use.

heading: One-line version
content:
### One-line version
> Built a multi-branch laundry POS with partial payments and 3-channel customer notifications, live with a paying client and planned for SaaS evolution.

---

heading: Tags
content:
## Tags

`web` `full-stack` `laravel` `react` `inertia.js` `paystack` `pos` `saas` `multi-branch` `notifications` `solo` `client` `ghana`

heading: Project: KGL Group Corporate Website
content:
# Project: KGL Group Corporate Website

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Live |
| **Period** | Early 2025 (approx. 2 months) |
| **Role** | Sole developer â€” full implementation from Figma to production |
| **Type** | Solo (client via Paper Merchant) |
| **Client / Organisation** | KGL Group |
| **Industry** | Enterprise / Conglomerate / ICT |
| **Confidential?** | No â€” publicly live |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

KGL Group â€” a major Ghanaian conglomerate with six subsidiaries spanning Fintech, Logistics, Agric, Property Development, and Commerce â€” needed a corporate website that reflected their scale and position. They had a design ready via Paper Merchant (a brand solutions agency) but needed a developer to implement it fully, integrate a CMS for ongoing content management, and deliver a production-ready site.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Implemented the full website from a Figma design provided by Paper Merchant â€” pixel-accurate, no design input from my side
- Built multi-page navigation covering all six subsidiaries, careers, press releases, gallery, and sponsorships sections
- Integrated Sanity CMS so the client can manage content independently after handover
- Deployed to production and handed over a fully functioning site with CMS access
- Delivered within approximately 2 months
- Quality of work led directly to a referral â€” Allied Ghana came through the same Paper Merchant relationship

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Next.js
- Sanity CMS
- TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS

**Approach / methodology:**

Implementation-focused project â€” the design direction was fully defined by Paper Merchant's Figma file. My job was accurate, performant implementation with a CMS layer that a non-technical team could use for ongoing updates. Used Next.js for performance and SSG/SSR capabilities, Sanity for structured content management across the multi-page site.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Delivery time | ~2 months | From Figma to production |
| Subsidiaries covered | 6 | Fintech, Logistics, Agric, Property, Commerce + Group |
| CMS sections | Multiple | Careers, press releases, gallery, sponsorships |
| Referral generated | Yes | Allied Ghana project followed directly |

**In plain language:**
Delivered a production corporate website for one of Ghana's top-ranked ICT conglomerates in approximately two months. The quality of the work earned a direct referral to a second enterprise client.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

- Large multi-page site with many distinct content types â€” careers, press, gallery, subsidiaries, sponsorships all needed separate CMS schemas
- Maintaining pixel accuracy across a complex Figma design at production quality
- Setting up Sanity CMS schemas to match the content model across a multi-section site so the client could self-manage after handover

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

Earning the referral. The KGL Group project came in, was delivered cleanly and on time, and the same agency introduced us to Allied Ghana without us having to ask. That's the best signal that the work was genuinely good.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

I'd establish a clearer content handover process earlier â€” documenting which sections are CMS-managed vs hardcoded would have saved time during the final handover and training phase.

---

heading: Artifacts & evidence
content:
## Artifacts & evidence

| Type | Description | Link / Location | Public? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live link | kglgroup.com.gh | https://kglgroup.com.gh | Yes |

---

heading: How to pitch this project
content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a multi-section Next.js + TypeScript + Sanity CMS corporate site for a six-subsidiary conglomerate. Implemented from Figma with structured content schemas covering careers, press releases, gallery, and sponsorships. Delivered to production in ~2 months.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
Built the corporate website for KGL Group, one of Ghana's largest ICT conglomerates. Delivered on time, with a content management system so their team can update it independently.

heading: One-line version
content:
### One-line version
> Built the corporate website for KGL Group â€” Ghana's top-ranked ICT conglomerate â€” in 2 months, earning a direct referral to a second enterprise client.

---

content:

heading: Project: Allied Ghana Corporate Website
content:
# Project: Allied Ghana Corporate Website

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Live |
| **Period** | ~Early 2025 â€“ January 2026 (~1 year) |
| **Role** | Sole developer â€” full implementation from Figma to production |
| **Type** | Solo (referral via Paper Merchant / KGL Group) |
| **Client / Organisation** | Allied Ghana (Allied Oil) |
| **Industry** | Energy / Fuel & Lubricants |
| **Confidential?** | No â€” publicly live |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

Allied Ghana â€” a Ghanaian fuel and lubricants company operating since 1998, with partnerships including Afton Chemical, Castrol, Toyota, and Mobil â€” needed a corporate website. They had a Figma design via Paper Merchant but went through an extended, iterative feedback cycle before reaching a final approved design. The project required patience, professionalism, and sustained responsiveness across nearly a year of client-driven changes.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Implemented the full website from a Figma design provided by Paper Merchant
- Built product and services pages, a station locator, fuel pricing display, and the Allied+ loyalty card section
- Integrated Sanity CMS for ongoing content management
- Implemented partnership content sections (Afton Chemical, Castrol, Toyota, Mobil)
- Navigated approximately one year of design change cycles â€” staying professional and responsive throughout
- Delivered and launched to production in January 2026

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Next.js
- Sanity CMS
- TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS

**Approach / methodology:**

Same stack as KGL Group â€” Next.js + Sanity CMS + TypeScript + Tailwind, implemented from a Paper Merchant Figma design. The technical complexity was moderate; the real challenge was project management. The client requested frequent design changes across nearly a year. Managed this by staying communicative, implementing changes accurately, and maintaining the relationship through what was a genuinely long and iterative process.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Delivery | January 2026 | After ~1 year of iterations |
| CMS sections | Multiple | Products, pricing, station locator, partnerships |
| Client relationship | Maintained | Despite extended change cycles |
| Source | Referral | From KGL Group / Paper Merchant |

**In plain language:**
Delivered a full corporate website for a long-established Ghanaian energy company, navigating nearly a year of client-driven design changes while keeping the relationship intact and delivering to production.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

This was not a technically complex project â€” it was a relationship and endurance challenge. Frequent design changes, long feedback cycles, and a drawn-out timeline tested professionalism and patience more than engineering skill. Keeping the project alive and the client happy across that timeline was the real work.

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

Finishing it. A lot of freelancers would have let a project like this die or damaged the relationship under that timeline. Delivering it cleanly â€” and leaving the client satisfied â€” is what matters here.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

Establish a clearer change management process at the start â€” a defined number of revision rounds, with additional rounds scoped and quoted separately. This would have protected both sides and reduced the timeline significantly.

---

heading: Artifacts & evidence
content:
## Artifacts & evidence

| Type | Description | Link / Location | Public? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live link | alliedghana.com | https://alliedghana.com | Yes |

---

content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a Next.js + TypeScript + Sanity CMS corporate website for a fuel and lubricants company â€” station locator, product pages, loyalty card section, and partnership content. Implemented from Figma, delivered to production after an extended client iteration cycle.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
Delivered the corporate website for Allied Ghana, an energy company with 25+ years in the Ghanaian market, navigating a year of design revisions while maintaining the client relationship throughout.

content:
### One-line version

heading: Project: Kitchen Comfort E-commerce Storefront
content:
# Project: Kitchen Comfort E-commerce Storefront

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Live â€” handed over to client |
| **Period** | August 2025 |
| **Role** | Solo developer â€” designed and built end-to-end |
| **Type** | Solo |
| **Client / Organisation** | Kitchen Comfort |
| **Industry** | E-commerce / Kitchenware / Retail |
| **Confidential?** | No â€” publicly live |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

A Ghanaian kitchenware brand needed a production-ready e-commerce store to sell their products online. They had no existing digital sales channel and needed a full storefront with product management, order handling, payments, and a way to manage inventory â€” all with minimal ongoing technical involvement from them after handover.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Designed and built the full e-commerce platform from scratch â€” no pre-existing design handed to me
- Structured 62 products across 8 categories with variant support and sale pricing
- Built a full admin dashboard for the client to manage products, orders, and stock independently
- Integrated Paystack for payments
- Set up Cloudinary for image storage and optimisation
- Configured Zoho Mail for transactional emails (order confirmations, notifications)
- Handed over to client after launch with full admin access

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Next.js
- Prisma ORM
- PostgreSQL (Neon â€” serverless)
- Paystack
- Cloudinary
- Zoho Mail

**Approach / methodology:**

Full-stack Next.js application with a Prisma + PostgreSQL backend. Used Neon for serverless PostgreSQL hosting to keep infrastructure simple and cost-effective for a small business client. Cloudinary handles all product image uploads and delivery with automatic optimisation. Zoho Mail handles transactional email without requiring a dedicated mail server.

The admin dashboard is the key non-technical requirement â€” the client needed to be able to add products, update stock, and manage orders without developer help after handover. Built this as a first-class part of the product, not an afterthought.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Products | 62 | Across 8 categories |
| Orders processed | 45 | Since launch |
| Orders delivered | 23 | As of last update |
| Categories | 8 | With variant and sale pricing support |

**In plain language:**
A fully live e-commerce store that has processed 45 real orders since launch. Built, delivered, and handed over â€” the client runs it independently using the admin dashboard.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

- Building a genuinely usable admin dashboard for a non-technical client â€” it needed to be simple enough that they'd actually use it
- Managing product variants and sale pricing in a clean data model
- Configuring the full transactional email and image pipeline so everything works automatically after handover without ongoing developer involvement

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

The handover. The client runs the store independently â€” they add products, manage orders, and handle stock without calling me. That was the goal and it worked.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

I'd spend more time on the initial product data structure with the client â€” some category and variant decisions had to be revisited after launch as the client's needs became clearer.

---

heading: Artifacts & evidence
content:
## Artifacts & evidence

| Type | Description | Link / Location | Public? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live link | kitchen-comfort.com | https://kitchen-comfort.com | Yes |

---

content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a full-stack Next.js + Prisma + PostgreSQL (Neon) e-commerce platform with Paystack integration, Cloudinary image handling, and Zoho Mail transactional email. 62 products, variant support, sale pricing, full admin dashboard. 45 orders processed since launch.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
Built a complete online store for a Ghanaian kitchenware brand â€” 62 products, real payments, and a self-service admin panel. 45 orders processed since launch, with the client managing it independently.

heading: One-line version
content:
### One-line version
> Built a production e-commerce storefront for a Ghanaian kitchenware brand â€” 62 products, Paystack payments, 45 orders processed since launch.

---

heading: Project: CSIR Real-Time Noise Monitoring Dashboard
content:
# Project: CSIR Real-Time Noise Monitoring Dashboard

---

heading: Overview
content:
## Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status** | Completed â€” handed over |
| **Period** | June 2024 â€“ August 2024 (3 months) |
| **Role** | Frontend developer (intern) |
| **Type** | Team (me + backend researcher/developer at CSIR) |
| **Client / Organisation** | Council for Scientific and Industrial Research â€” Institute of Industrial Research (CSIR-INSTI) |
| **Industry** | Government / Research / Environmental monitoring |
| **Confidential?** | Partially â€” government research project; screenshots/details available but not publicly deployed |

---

heading: The problem
content:
## The problem

CSIR-INSTI had deployed a network of IoT noise monitoring devices across the city to measure environmental noise levels in real time. The raw sensor data was being collected by a backend system but there was no frontend to visualise it â€” no way for researchers to see which devices were active, what noise levels they were reporting, or where they were located geographically.

---

heading: What I did
content:
## What I did

- Built the complete frontend dashboard for the noise monitoring system
- Consumed real-time IoT sensor data via MQTT protocol
- Displayed live decibel readings per device, updating in real time
- Implemented an interactive map showing all device locations across the city
- Built map clustering so the dashboard remains usable as the number of devices scales up
- Integrated with the backend system built by the CSIR researcher/developer
- Handed over a fully working frontend at the end of the 3-month internship

---

heading: How I did it
content:
## How I did it

**Tools & technologies:**
- Next.js (first project using this framework)
- MQTT (real-time IoT data streaming)
- Map library with clustering support

**Approach / methodology:**

The backend pipeline was already built by the CSIR researcher â€” my job was to consume the MQTT data stream and present it usefully. MQTT is a lightweight publish/subscribe protocol designed for IoT â€” devices publish readings to topics, and the dashboard subscribes and updates in real time. The map clustering implementation was designed with future scale in mind: as more devices are deployed across the city, the map stays readable by grouping nearby sensors at lower zoom levels.

---

heading: Results & impact
content:
## Results & impact

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Devices on map | Multiple | Live across the city |
| Real-time update | Yes | Via MQTT subscription |
| Supervisor recognition | Yes | Praised MQTT integration and clustering |
| Personal impact | High | Introduced me to Next.js â€” used in every project since |

**In plain language:**
Delivered a live dashboard for a government research institution that turns raw IoT sensor data into an interactive, real-time map. The supervisor specifically praised the MQTT integration and clustering â€” and this project introduced me to Next.js, which became my primary framework.

---

heading: What made this hard
content:
## What made this hard

- MQTT was a completely new protocol â€” had to learn it from scratch during the internship
- Next.js was also new â€” this was my first project using it
- Real-time data introduces complexity around state updates, connection handling, and keeping the UI in sync with the data stream
- Map clustering required understanding how to balance detail vs readability at different zoom levels

---

heading: What I'm proud of
content:
## What I'm proud of

Learning two new things (MQTT + Next.js) in a real production context, on a government research project, and delivering something the supervisor specifically called out as well done. This project set the technical direction for everything that came after.

---

heading: What I'd do differently
content:
## What I'd do differently

I'd spend more time on connection resilience â€” handling MQTT disconnects and reconnects more gracefully. In a production monitoring context, the dashboard needs to recover cleanly when the connection drops.

---

content:

|---|---|---|---|

heading: How to pitch this project
content: ## How to pitch this project

heading: For a technical audience
content:
### For a technical audience
Built a real-time IoT data dashboard in Next.js consuming live sensor readings via MQTT from a city-wide noise monitoring network. Implemented interactive map with device locations, live dB readouts, and clustering for scale. Delivered during a 3-month internship at CSIR-INSTI.

heading: For a business / non-technical audience
content:
### For a business / non-technical audience
Built a live dashboard for a government research institution to monitor environmental noise levels across the city in real time â€” turning raw IoT sensor data into a visual, interactive map.

heading: One-line version
content:
### One-line version
> Built a real-time IoT noise monitoring dashboard for CSIR-INSTI â€” live MQTT data, interactive city map, and clustering for scale.
